claimed_contribution: Provides a structural framework for modeling customer retention in which loyalty rewards and other marketing instruments are evaluated in a dynamic discrete-choice setting.
Shows how to estimate loyalty-program effects when rewards depend on cumulative purchases over time, while jointly accounting for short-term factors such as prices, e-mail coupons, and shipping fees.
Demonstrates policy-simulation use of the estimated model to compare long-term effects of alternative promotions for multicampaign direct marketing decisions.
context: The empirical setting is an online retailer specializing in nonperishable grocery and drugstore items. The loyalty program awards frequent-flyer miles when annual cumulative spending crosses thresholds ($1,000, $1,500, $2,000), and points do not carry over across annual cycles.
design_and_data: The paper models weekly customer choices among no order, small, medium, and large orders as a finite-horizon (52-week) dynamic optimization problem. State variables include cumulative spending, recency, price level, e-mail coupon availability, shipping-fee schedule, and expected future prices. The full database covers more than 30,000 customers over 13 months (1997-1998), and the estimation sample contains 1,058 customers selected for meaningful transaction histories (at least two purchases in first 5 weeks and at least 30 weeks observed). Estimation uses maximum likelihood with backward recursion, includes latent-class heterogeneity, and uses simulation/policy experiments (including 100,000-customer simulations).
main_findings: Dynamic specifications fit the data better than myopic specifications, and the loyalty reward effect is positive and statistically significant in the dynamic model.
In the best-fitting two-segment dynamic model, the loyalty effect is concentrated in one larger segment, while the other segment shows little reward response.

Policy simulations indicate that removing loyalty rewards lowers weekly purchase incidence (19.7% to 19.2%), reduces mean annual orders (10.25 to 9.98), and decreases average annual customer revenue (from $647 to $634).
one_line_summary: Using online grocery retail panel data, the paper estimates a dynamic discrete-choice model and finds that a threshold-based loyalty program increases repeat purchasing and annual revenue for a substantial share of customers.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior empirical loyalty-program work that often used single-period switching models by modeling customer choices as forward-looking dynamic optimization under cumulative reward thresholds. It also integrates loyalty incentives and short-term marketing actions in one empirical retention model.
research_gap: Prior empirical evidence on loyalty-program effectiveness is limited and contradictory, and much of it is based on single-period models that do not represent cumulative, time-dependent reward incentives. The paper addresses the need for an empirical model that captures dynamic response to loyalty rewards while jointly estimating short-term promotion effects on retention.
research_question: How much does a cumulative-purchase loyalty program affect customer retention and purchasing over time, relative to short-term promotions and other marketing instruments? The paper also asks whether customer behavior in this setting is better explained by forward-looking dynamic decision making than by myopic choice.
